body:
{
  "title": "Enterprise Land-and-Expand",
  "compiled_truth": "Enterprise land-and-expand is the go-to-market thesis that underpins how infrastructure startups penetrate large organizations without requiring top-down procurement cycles. The core idea is deceptively simple: you sell to a single team, a single project, sometimes even a single engineer — and you let the product prove itself. Once ROI is demonstrated in a contained environment, expansion becomes a pull motion rather than a push. For companies building deterministic data infrastructure, this pattern is especially potent because the value compounds as more systems and teams adopt the primitives.\n\nThe thesis matters for Anchor Codec because our single-pass primitives touch data at such a fundamental layer that adoption by one team creates immediate adjacency to others. A platform engineering group deploying our compression pipeline for a logging project inevitably surfaces questions from the security team about authentication, and from the networking team about transmission. Each new surface area becomes a new expansion vector.\n\n[Tatum Andersen](people/tatum-andersen) has been one of the most vocal internal advocates for structuring our pricing and packaging around this motion. Her argument — which she's made repeatedly since mid-2027 — is that usage-based pricing with a generous free tier for small teams is the wedge, and that enterprise agreements should only be introduced once three or more teams within the same org are active. This is a deliberate inversion of the traditional enterprise sales playbook, and it's caused some tension with board members who want to see larger upfront contracts.\n\n[Crestline Capital](companies/crestline-capital) has pushed back on this approach at times, preferring to see faster revenue recognition. But the data from our [Vellum Systems](companies/vellum-systems) deployment tells a compelling story: what started as a single integration within their edge compute division expanded to four business units within nine months, with ARR growing roughly 11x from the initial contract. [Jasper Moreau](people/jasper-moreau) at Vellum championed the internal expansion and has become something of a reference customer archetype for us.\n\nThe risk with land-and-expand is that you can get stuck in the \"land\" phase — lots of small deployments that never graduate to org-wide adoption. Monitoring expansion signals (cross-team API calls, rising data volumes, internal Slack mentions) is critcal. We've started building lightweight tooling to track these signals, though it's still early. The thesis only works if the product genuinely delivers outsized value in the initial deployment; otherwise expansion stalls and you're left with a portfolio of small accounts that churn.",
  "timeline": [
    "- **2026-08-14** | Initial discussion of land-and-expand pricing model during offsite with founding team and early advisors.",
    "- **2026-11-02** | First \"wedge\" deployment at [Vellum Systems](companies/vellum-systems) — single project within their edge compute division, led by [Jasper Moreau](people/jasper-moreau).",
    "- **2027-01-19** | [Tatum Andersen](people/tatum-andersen) circulates internal memo arguing for usage-based pricing as the default for sub-10-seat teams.",
    "- **2027-04-07** | [Crestline Capital](companies/crestline-capital) board meeting includes heated debate on land-and-expand vs. top-down enterprise sales motion.",
    "- **2027-06-22** | Vellum expansion hits second business unit; Jasper Moreau introduces Anchor to their data platform team.",
    "- **2027-09-11** | Third Vellum division onboards. Internal case study drafted for sales enablement.",
    "- **2027-11-30** | Tatum presents revised packaging tiers at all-hands — introduces \"Team\", \"Department\", and \"Organization\" plans explicitly designed around expansion triggers.",
    "- **2028-02-15** | Vellum signs org-wide enterprise agreement. ARR from the account reaches ~11x the original contract value.",
    "- **2028-05-03** | Expansion signal monitoring tooling (internal codename \"Sonar\") enters alpha testing with three pilot accounts."
  ]
}
